title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Surge in LCFS Trading Volumes and Prices Amid CARB Amendments
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(26th Aug â€“ 01st September)
  • A total of 207,765 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 92.4% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a 1.24x times rise as 715,563 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price was $53.54/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, a rise of 4.00%from the past week; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 7.33% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 3.59% WoW to reach $55.35MT last week; on 03rd Sep, it was $57.00/MT.
  • In the RFS market, D3 RIN prices increased by 0.90% WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices also saw a rise of 3.64% each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

As the comment period for CARB's proposed amendments concluded on August 27th, the market has responded swiftly, as evidenced by a significant uptick in trading activity. This market reaction suggests that the increased stringency introduced by CARB, along with any further amendments resulting from recent public comments, could lead to greater price stabilization. The CA LCFS market has experienced a prolonged decline in prices due to the heavy influx of renewable diesel, which has generated a substantial number of credits. However, with the newly implemented stringent measures and limitations on certain feedstocks, credit generation is expected to decrease. The last week of August notably witnessed a significant surge in trading volumes alongside rising prices, reflecting the market's positive response to these developments. Various stakeholders have expressed support for these limitations. The Center for Biological Diversity has pointed out that the most recent amendment proposed by CARB aims to "provide credits for biomass-based diesel produced from virgin soybean oil and canola oil for up to 20% of annual biomass-based diesel reported on a company-wide basis." While this introduces a limitation on crediting for these two problematic feedstocks, the Center views the measure as too narrow in scope. They are urging CARB to increase the cap and implement stronger restrictions to prevent credit generation from renewable diesel production based on these feedstocks. Moreover, the measure still allows for some crediting of soybean and canola oils and does not restrict the expanded use of other crop-based feedstocks, such as corn and other grains. The Sunflower Alliance has similarly expressed that while the cap on canola oil and virgin soybean oil is a positive step, it should be broadened and implemented more swiftly. In contrast, the RNG Coalition has welcomed CARB's 9% step down in the CI target, viewing it as a positive development and a strong indication that California is advancing this proven emissions reduction program.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_587311" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.00% WoW rise from $51.48 to $53.54 last week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 7.33% compared to the previous week. A total of 207,765 credits were exchanged in Type 1 trades, marking 92.4% rise WoW. The transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades also saw a 1.24x times rise last week to reach 715,563 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_587312" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 3.59% WoW, reaching $55.35/MT. On 03rd Sep, the average spot price was $57.00/MT, up from $43.00/MT, the lowest point in July. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $57.50/MT as of 03rd Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_587313" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 43 trades were reported in the market, with six high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $69.40 for two 40,000 credits of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $43.00 per credit of Type 3, reported at a volume of 2,826. The largest volume transfer of 1,20,000 credits in Type 2, was undertaken for a price of $54.25/MT, and the smallest volume transfers of 1,007 credits, a Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $53.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_587314" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] As August comes to a close, trading volumes have significantly decreased to 1.90 MMT, down from the 5.21 MMT recorded in July. While July saw trading volumes rebound to April levels, August experienced a rise in Type 1 prices, which had remained relatively stable in June and July. The proposed increase of the CI reduction target to 9% for 2025, up from the original 5% annual target, may renew stakeholder interest. However, the cap on renewable diesel and biodiesel made from soybean and canola oil could raise concerns among some producers.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_587315" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 26th Aug- 01st Sep, total VPT recorded a 24.44% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 0.90% WoW, while D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices saw a rise of 3.64% each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • California Scores Big Clean Transportation Boost from Biden-Harris Administration. California, along with Oregon and Washington, have secured $102 million to install charging stations and hydrogen fueling stations for zero-emissions trucks along Interstate 5 and other key freight corridors. An additional $47 million is going to other entities across the state for electric vehicle infrastructure. This is on top of more than $250 million recently announced for upgrading airports across California and supporting sustainable aviation fuel.
  • Growth Energy: California LCFS Updates Stack the Deck against American Biofuels. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, submitted new comments to the California Air Resources Board (CARB) today, criticizing the boardâ€™s latest updates to Californiaâ€™s Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) for unfairly singling out American biofuels in a way that will make it hard for them to generate credits under the program. Specifically, Growth Energy objected to a proposed â€œsustainability certificationâ€ requirement, calling it â€œan unfair and unnecessary double penalty for corn starch bioethanol.â€
  • California Launches $500 Million Funding Effort to Accelerate Switch to Zero-Emission School Buses. California is launching a new statewide funding program that will put 1,000 new, zero-emission school buses on the road, furthering the stateâ€™s lead in having the cleanest buses in the nation. The program will distribute $500 million to school districts and other educational entities to replace aging school buses with zero-emission vehicles and purchase needed infrastructure. The Zero-Emissions School Bus and Infrastructure (ZESBI) program is accepting funding applications through Sept. 30.
  • Dimerco Partners with Acer and Cathay Cargo to Drive Sustainability through Sustainable Aviation Fuel. Dimerco Express Group (TWSE: 5609) is proud to announce its partnership with Acer (TWSE: 2353) and Cathay Cargo (Cathay Pacific, SEHK: 293) in a groundbreaking initiative to reduce carbon emissions in aviation through the use of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF). As part of Dimercoâ€™s commitment to sustainability and reducing the environmental impact of logistics, this agreement will support the adoption of SAF on Cathay Cargoâ€™s flights starting in 2024.
  • Biden-Harris Administration Awards More than $12 Million to 35 State, Local and Tribal Governments to Support Clean Energy Local Projects and Lower Energy Costs. As part of the Biden-Harris Administrationâ€™s Investing in America agenda, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) today announced its latest round of awards under the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Block Grant (EECBG) Program. The EECBG Program will distribute $12.62 million to 32 local governments, two states, and one Tribe to improve energy efficiency, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, and lower overall energy use. Funded by President Bidenâ€™s Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and managed by DOEâ€™s Office of State and Community Energy Programs, the EECBG program makes formula grants to States, territories, the District of Columbia and eligible Tribal and local governments. The program funding is highly flexible: recipients may apply it towards 14 eligible categories of clean energy projects and programs that will help them reduce energy consumption, promote energy efficiency, and advance environmental justice.
  • SAFFiRE Renewables Celebrates Groundbreaking of Pilot Plant Supporting SAF. SAFFiRE Renewables LLC broke ground on its pilot plant at Conestoga Energyâ€™s Arkalon Energy facility in Liberal, Kansas, marking a significant step in SAFFiREâ€™s journey to transform corn stover into an abundant low carbon feedstock for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF). Once finished, the plant will be capable of processing 10 tons of corn stover every day, yielding cellulosic ethanol that can be upgraded into SAF with a carbon footprint at least 83% lower than conventional jet fuel.
  • Natural Resources Canada Invests in Canadian Team Advancing Research to Support Domestic Clean Fuels Production Capacity and Energy Innovation. Canadian Nuclear Laboratories (CNL), Canadaâ€™s premier nuclear science and technology laboratory, in partnership with Expander Energy Inc., FuelCell Energy, Nuclear Promise X, and St Marys Cement, are pleased to announce they have been awarded $4.9 million from Natural Resources Canadaâ€™s Clean Fuels Fund (CFF) and Energy Innovation Program (EIP) to support two new projects. The team is focused on using cutting-edge technologies to produce ready-to-deploy low carbon intensity synthetic diesel and Sustainable Aviation Fuel that could serve Canadaâ€™s transport sector and support Canadaâ€™s zero emissions future.
